content.text: Branching creates parallel lines of development; merging combines them back. In agent repositories, branching is the primary mechanism for safely iterating on prompts, tool definitions, and policy configurations without destabilising the production agent — and every merge is gated by an eval suite rather than just a linter.
in Git is a lightweight, movable pointer to a specific commit. When you create a branch, Git writes a new file under
containing the current commit SHA — no data is duplicated. The default branch is called
and acts as the source of truth for production deployments. Every other branch is a divergence from some point on that history.

combines two branch tips into a new commit with two parents. Git supports three integration strategies: (1) a standard merge commit that preserves full branch history, (2) a squash merge that collapses the branch into one commit before adding it to the target, and (3) a rebase that replays commits on top of the target to create a linear history. Each strategy trades history clarity against conflict surface area differently.

git checkout -b feat/tool-approval-flow
# edit prompts and handlers
git add .
git commit -m "Add human-in-the-loop approval before delete tool"
git checkout main
git merge feat/tool-approval-flow

Agentic systems are uniquely sensitive to unreviewed changes. A subtle prompt edit can cause an agent to invoke destructive tools without confirmation, bypass safety policies, or hallucinate structured output that breaks downstream parsers. Branching enforces a clean separation between in-progress experiments and validated behaviour: unreleased prompt or config changes live exclusively on a feature branch until an eval gate on CI confirms the change improves — or at minimum does not regress — the agent's measured performance on a golden test set.

Branching also enables parallel workstreams on a team. One engineer can iterate on retrieval prompt quality while another patches a tool-safety bug, without either blocking the other. The hotfix branch can be merged to production the same day it is written; the longer prompt experiment lands weeks later — both paths are independent until the moment of merge.
— experiments cannot corrupt main; a broken prompt on a feature branch does not reach production
— multiple prompt or tool iterations develop simultaneously without serialising the team
— one branch typically maps to one pull request, making the scope of review clear and bounded
— CI can require a passing eval score before allowing merge, providing a quantitative quality bar for non-deterministic agent behaviour
— reverting a squash merge commit undoes the entire feature atomically, without hunting through interleaved commits
— branch names encode intent; the merge commit message records what changed, eval scores, and reviewer sign-off
— model-swap experiments, A/B prompt variants, and architecture refactors can live on separate branches simultaneously
— two branches editing the same prompt file create a conflict requiring human judgment to resolve correctly
— the longer a branch stays open, the more main moves ahead, making the eventual merge progressively more expensive
— rewriting commit history on a shared branch forces collaborators to reset their local copies; only rebase unshared branches
— stale experiment branches accumulate on the remote over months; automated cleanup is necessary
— switching between branches mid-task requires saving work with
— merging without running evals is as dangerous as deploying untested code; the eval suite is the test harness for non-deterministic agent behaviour
Always start from a freshly pulled main to minimise the gap between your branch point and the current tip. Use
to register the upstream tracking reference.

git switch main
git pull --ff-only origin main
git switch -c feat/rag-v2--semantic-chunking
git push -u origin feat/rag-v2--semantic-chunking

A standard merge creates a new commit with two parents — the tips of both branches. The full branch history is preserved in the graph. Use this for hotfix branches where you want an explicit, datable record that a specific patch was applied.

git switch main
git merge --no-ff hotfix/tool-safety--auth-check
# produces a merge commit with both parents visible in git log --graph

Squash merge collapses all branch commits into a single staged changeset, then lets you write one clean commit message before adding it to main. WIP commits like "tweak prompt again" disappear from main's history, replaced by one commit that documents what changed and why, with eval results included in the body.

git switch main
git merge --squash feat/rag-v2--semantic-chunking
git commit -m "feat(rag-v2): semantic chunking raises retrieval F1 from 0.81 to 0.89"

Rebase replays your commits on top of the target branch tip, rewriting commit SHAs to produce a linear history with no merge commit. Use rebase only to update a local, unshared branch with changes from main — never force-push a rebased branch that others have checked out.

git fetch origin
git rebase origin/main
# if conflicts: resolve, git add <file>, git rebase --continue
When Git cannot auto-merge a file it inserts conflict markers:

. For source code either side may be correct. For prompt files the right resolution often combines intent from both sides — something only a human familiar with both branches can judge. After resolving, re-run the full eval suite to confirm the merged prompt behaves as expected.

git switch main && git pull
git merge feat/new-system-prompt
# CONFLICT in prompts/system.md
# edit file, remove <<<<<<< markers
git add prompts/system.md
git commit
# always re-run evals after resolving a prompt conflict:
python3 evals/run_suite.py

The diagram below shows the lifecycle of two branches running in parallel against a main branch. A feature branch (green) squash-merges after CI approval; a hotfix branch (amber) uses a merge commit to preserve an explicit record of the fix.
per agent capability or prompt change — target lifetime of one to five working days
for A/B prompt comparisons or model swaps — keep open until eval results are conclusive, then merge the winner or discard
off main for urgent tool-safety or output-format patches — fast-track review and merge with a merge commit for traceability
) for stabilisation before a tag — only bug fixes flow in; no new features
committing directly to main in any shared repo — even solo developers benefit from the PR review step as a self-check
branches that span multiple unrelated concerns — split them so each PR has a single, clearly reviewable purpose
Consistent branch names communicate intent at a glance and enable automation. A useful convention for agent repos:

Enable branch protection on GitHub or GitLab: require at least one PR approval, require all CI checks to pass including the eval gate, require branches to be up to date before merging, and disallow direct and force pushes. These rules make the review-and-validation pipeline structurally mandatory.
Step 3 — add a CI eval gate

Add a CI job that runs the eval suite on every PR branch. Configure a minimum acceptable score — 0.85 on the golden test set is a reasonable starting point. Block merge if the score regresses. Store the score in the merge commit message for future auditing during incident post-mortems.

Configure GitHub to auto-delete branches after merge. Supplement with a weekly scheduled script that prunes stale remote branches — those with no push activity for more than 30 days. The branch manager script in the Examples section handles both tasks.

The script below checks for merge conflicts and verifies the eval gate before a branch is considered ready. Run it locally before opening a PR or integrate it as a required CI step that blocks merge on failure.
This module enforces naming conventions when creating branches and automates post-merge cleanup of stale local and remote refs.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""branch_manager.py — enforce naming conventions and automate lifecycle tasks.

Naming convention:
  <type>/<scope>--<slug>
  e.g.  feat/rag-retrieval--semantic-chunking-v3
        hotfix/tool-approval--missing-auth-check
        experiment/gpt4o-mini--latency-benchmark
        release/1.5.0--stabilisation
"""
import subprocess
import re
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum


class BranchType(Enum):
    FEATURE    = "feat"
    EXPERIMENT = "experiment"
    HOTFIX     = "hotfix"
    RELEASE    = "release"


_VALID_SLUG = re.compile(r"^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9-]*$")
_VALID_NAME = re.compile(
    r"^(feat|experiment|hotfix|release)/[a-z0-9][a-z0-9-]*--[a-z0-9][a-z0-9-]*$"
)


@dataclass
class Branch:
    type: BranchType
    scope: str   # component/area, e.g. "rag-retrieval"
    slug: str    # short description, e.g. "semantic-chunking-v3"

    def name(self) -> str:
        return f"{self.type.value}/{self.scope}--{self.slug}"

    def validate(self) -> bool:
        if not _VALID_SLUG.match(self.scope):
            raise ValueError(f"Invalid scope: {self.scope!r}")
        if not _VALID_SLUG.match(self.slug):
            raise ValueError(f"Invalid slug: {self.slug!r}")
        return bool(_VALID_NAME.match(self.name()))


def _run(args: list[str], **kw) -> subprocess.CompletedProcess:
    return subprocess.run(args, check=True, text=True, **kw)


def create_branch(branch: Branch, base: str = "main") -> None:
    """Switch to base, pull latest, create and push the new branch."""
    assert branch.validate(), f"Invalid branch name: {branch.name()!r}"
    _run(["git", "switch", base])
    _run(["git", "pull", "--ff-only"])
    _run(["git", "switch", "-c", branch.name()])
    _run(["git", "push", "-u", "origin", branch.name()])
    print(f"Created and pushed: {branch.name()}")


def list_merged_branches(main_branch: str = "main") -> list[str]:
    """Return local branches already merged into main (excluding main itself)."""
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["git", "branch", "--merged", main_branch, "--format=%(refname:short)"],
        capture_output=True, text=True, check=True,
    )
    return [
        b.strip() for b in result.stdout.splitlines()
        if b.strip() and b.strip() != main_branch
    ]


def delete_merged_branches(remote: str = "origin", dry_run: bool = False) -> None:
    """Delete local and remote refs for branches already merged into main."""
    merged = list_merged_branches()
    if not merged:
        print("No merged branches to delete.")
        return
    for name in merged:
        if dry_run:
            print(f"  [DRY RUN] Would delete: {name}")
            continue
        _run(["git", "branch", "-d", name])
        _run(["git", "push", remote, "--delete", name])
        print(f"Deleted: {name}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Create a new experiment branch
    b = Branch(BranchType.EXPERIMENT, "rag-retrieval", "semantic-chunking-v3")
    create_branch(b)

    # Preview stale branches before deleting
    delete_merged_branches(dry_run=True)
The complete sequence from branch creation through squash merge, tagging, and cleanup — suitable as a team runbook or a shell alias library.

# Full branch lifecycle in an agent repo — reference commands

# 1. Start from a clean main
git switch main
git pull --ff-only origin main

# 2. Create a feature branch (naming convention enforced)
git switch -c feat/tool-catalog--approval-flow

# 3. Work in small, focused commits
git add prompts/tool_approval.txt config/tool_policy.json
git commit -m "Add human-in-the-loop approval step for delete-class tools"

git add tests/test_tool_policy.py
git commit -m "Test: delete tools require explicit approval before execution"

# 4. Stay current with main via rebase (safe while branch is local / unshared)
git fetch origin main
git rebase origin/main
# If a conflict arises during rebase:
#   git status               # identify conflicting files
#   # edit conflict markers; for prompt files involve a human reviewer
#   git add <resolved-file>
#   git rebase --continue

# 5. Push and open PR
git push -u origin feat/tool-catalog--approval-flow
# CI must pass: lint + unit tests + eval gate (score >= 0.85)
# Two reviewer approvals required (branch protection rule)

# 6. Squash-merge after approval (single clean commit on main)
git switch main
git pull --ff-only origin main
git merge --squash feat/tool-catalog--approval-flow
git commit -m "feat(tool-catalog): add human-in-the-loop approval for delete-class tools

- Approval required before any tool with destructive side-effects executes
- Policy enforced in ToolPolicy.check(); configurable per role
- Eval: all 47 approval-flow test cases passing (score 0.93)"
git push origin main

# 7. Tag release (if this completes a version)
git tag -a v1.5.0 -m "Release 1.5.0 — tool approval flow"
git push origin v1.5.0

# 8. Clean up branch refs
git branch -d feat/tool-catalog--approval-flow
git push origin --delete feat/tool-catalog--approval-flow

# ---- Hotfix workflow (parallel path, merge commit for traceability) ----------
git switch main
git switch -c hotfix/tool-safety--missing-auth-check
# ... one or two focused commits ...
git push -u origin hotfix/tool-safety--missing-auth-check

git switch main
git merge --no-ff hotfix/tool-safety--missing-auth-check \
    -m "hotfix(tool-safety): require auth header on all tool-invoke endpoints"
git push origin main

git branch -d hotfix/tool-safety--missing-auth-check
git push origin --delete hotfix/tool-safety--missing-auth-check
Consider a team building a RAG-powered support agent. On
the retrieval prompt uses fixed-size chunking and achieves an F1 of 0.81 on the golden eval set. An engineer creates
and over three working days makes eight commits — adjusting chunk boundaries, updating the embedding model call, and revising the prompt to describe the new retrieval context format.

Each push triggers CI: lint, unit tests, and the full eval suite. On day two the score drops to 0.79 — below the 0.85 threshold — and the branch is blocked from merging. The engineer narrows the chunk overlap parameter and the score rises to 0.89. A PR is opened; two teammates review the prompt diff and the eval report attached automatically by CI.
After two approvals the branch is squash-merged to main:

feat(rag-v2): semantic chunking raises retrieval F1 from 0.81 to 0.89

- Replaced fixed-size splitter with RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter (512/64)
- Updated retrieval prompt: 'document fragment' instead of 'chunk'
- Eval: 47/47 golden cases pass; F1=0.89 (+0.08 vs main baseline)
Reviewed-by: Alice <alice@example.com>, Bob <bob@example.com>
is tagged from the squash commit SHA, and the agent worker image is rebuilt and deployed. If the new version causes a regression a week later,
on main undoes the entire change atomically.
— the branch may pass evals against an old tip; pulling first surfaces integration issues when they are cheapest to resolve
— collaborators who have checked out the branch find their local history diverged from the rewritten remote and must reset or re-clone
Resolving prompt conflicts without re-running evals
— silent prompt corruption is the most dangerous mistake in an agent repo; always re-evaluate after any conflict resolution
— clutter grows over months; 150 stale branches make
unusable and obscure which branches are genuinely active
Multiple unrelated concerns on one branch
— a PR that simultaneously updates the system prompt, changes the tool schema, and adds a new endpoint is impossible to review safely or revert cleanly
Skipping the PR step on solo projects
— even without a reviewer, writing a PR description surfaces assumptions and forces a re-read of the diff
— they drift from main and accumulate integration debt; prefer short-lived feature branches off main directly
Rebase or merge main into your feature branch at least once per day to stay current and catch integration issues when they are cheapest to fix
One concern per branch — if you notice a second problem while working, open a separate branch rather than fixing it in-line
Write the PR description before requesting review — it forces you to articulate what changed and why, and that description becomes the squash merge commit body
Document eval results in the PR description — include baseline score, branch score, number of test cases, and any regressions in sub-categories
Enable auto-delete of head branches after merge on GitHub so cleanup is automatic for every merged PR
Prefer squash merge for feature branches so main's log stays readable; use merge commit for hotfixes where traceability of the exact patch is paramount
Never force-push to a branch after someone else has checked it out — communicate and coordinate before rewriting history
Add a pre-push hook that runs a quick subset of evals locally to catch regressions before CI picks them up, reducing the feedback loop to seconds
When the agent repo contains many components — prompt libraries, tool servers, eval datasets, deployment configs — a full clone can be slow. Use
for CI clones where full history is unnecessary, and
(partial clone) to defer downloading large binary assets until needed. Sparse checkout (
) limits the working tree to only the directories relevant to the task, further reducing checkout time.

A repo with many contributors can accumulate hundreds of stale branches. Run a scheduled GitHub Actions workflow weekly: list branches with no push activity in 30 days using the GitHub API, post a comment on the originating PR asking for cleanup, and auto-delete branches older than 60 days with no open PR. This prevents the remote from becoming a graveyard of abandoned experiments that slow down fetch and confuse contributors.

As the eval suite grows, CI merge-gate times increase and can block the team. Mitigate with: (1) caching model inference results for unchanged prompts using a content-addressed cache keyed on prompt hash, (2) running only the subset of evals relevant to the changed files using affected-test detection, and (3) distributing the eval suite across multiple CI workers in parallel. Target under 10 minutes end-to-end for the merge gate to avoid blocking the team.
— branch protection rules, required status checks, auto-merge after approvals, PR templates for standardised eval reporting
for scripted PR workflows from CI
— hooks that run lint and quick eval checks before every push, catching issues before CI
) feed automated changelog generators and version bumps via
— a branching model formalising develop, feature, release, and hotfix branches; widely known but often heavier than trunk-based approaches need
events to run eval suites, post score comments on PRs, and enforce the merge gate automatically
Teams with parallel work; agent repos with eval gates
Experienced teams with comprehensive test coverage; very fast CI
Long release cycles; versioned shipped software
Graduated trust model; low-risk changes skip PR review

For most agent teams, feature branching with squash merges is the right default. It gives enough isolation for eval-gated prompt experiments while keeping branch lifetime short enough to avoid significant drift. Pure trunk-based development is viable only when feature flags can hide unfinished prompt changes from production — a pattern that does not map cleanly onto prompt files loaded holistically by an agent at startup.

: require pull request before merging (minimum one approval), require all status checks to pass including the eval gate, require branches to be up to date before merging, restrict force pushes, and restrict deletions. These rules make it structurally impossible to bypass the review and validation pipeline even accidentally.

and require signed commits on the protected branch. This provides a cryptographic proof that commits were authored by the claimed identity — important when your agent repo contains tool definitions or policy configs that affect production safety and should not be tampered with.

Enable GitHub's secret scanning and push protection. A developer might accidentally commit an API key or model credential on a feature branch. Push protection blocks the push before the secret reaches the remote; secret scanning alerts when one is found in existing history. Combine with a pre-commit hook running
for a local first line of defence.

The combination of branch names, PR descriptions with eval results, and squash merge commit bodies creates a durable audit trail: for any commit on main you can trace back to the PR, the reviewers, the eval scores at merge time, and the feature branch that originated the change. This is essential for incident post-mortems when an agent behaves unexpectedly in production.

Branches are alternate timelines of your codebase. Merging is the moment two timelines converge into one — and for an agent repo, the eval gate is the arbitration step that decides which timeline's behaviour survives into the merged future.
Branch to isolate agent experiments; merge only after the eval gate confirms no regression — the gate is the quality bar for non-deterministic behaviour
Understand the three merge strategies: merge commit for traceability, squash merge for clean history, rebase only for local unshared branches
Resolve prompt and config conflicts with human judgment, then re-run evals before committing — silent corruption is the main risk in an agent repo
Keep branches short-lived (days, not weeks) and synced with main to limit conflict surface area and integration cost
Protect main with required reviews and a passing eval score; delete merged branches automatically to keep the remote clean
The branch lifecycle — create, develop, validate, merge, cleanup — is the fundamental release process for safe, iterative agent development
